objections.location: Section 4 — COMPONENT ARCHITECTURE MAP (bottom of artifact)
objections.description: The artifact is truncated mid-sentence inside the Library tab panel definition. Sections 5 through the end (presumably covering: detail screen tree, api.js implementation, useFetch hook, design token values, animation/motion system, dark/light theme token definitions, skeleton dimension specs, accessibility audit checklist, and the remaining 3 key screens) are entirely missing. This is not a minor omission — the convergence criteria explicitly require 'at least 3 key screens', 'motion system', 'dark/light theme switching', and 'design token system' to be fully specified before any agent can APPROVE. The spec currently delivers ~60% of the required deliverables.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete the artifact in full. At minimum, the following sections must exist with implementation-ready detail: (1) Remainder of Section 4 — Library tab panel, Detail screen component tree, BottomSheet tree. (2) Section 5 — Design Token System with actual HSL values for every token in both dark and light modes. (3) Section 6 — Motion System with exact keyframe definitions, timing functions (cubic-bezier values), durations, and reduced-motion overrides. (4) Section 7 — api.js specification (timeout handling, retry logic, response parsing, error classification). (5) Section 8 — useFetch hook contract (loading/error/data states, AbortController lifecycle, stale-while-revalidate or not). (6) Section 9 — Screen-by-screen layout specifications for Home, Search/Detail, and Library with exact spacing, responsive breakpoint behaviors, and skeleton-to-content transition rules. (7) Section 10 — Accessibility compliance matrix (WCAG 2.1 AA checklist mapped to components).

objections.description: The spec uses `inert` as a JSX attribute on React elements (e.g., `inert={activeTab !== 'home'}`). React 18 does NOT forward the `inert` attribute to the DOM — it silently drops unknown boolean HTML attributes that aren't in its allowlist. This was only fixed in React 19. On React 18, every inactive tab panel will remain keyboard-navigable and screen-reader-visible, completely breaking the lazy-mount isolation model. This is a correctness bug that will ship silently.
objections.suggested_fix: Use a ref-based approach: `useEffect(() => { if (ref.current) { if (isInactive) ref.current.setAttribute('inert', ''); else ref.current.removeAttribute('inert'); } }, [isInactive])`. Apply the same pattern to the bottom sheet's inert management of <main> and <TabBar>. Document this as a known React 18 limitation in the spec with the exact workaround code. Alternatively, add a thin `<InertContainer active={boolean}>` wrapper component that encapsulates the ref logic.

objections.description: The build command references `src/main.jsx` as the entrypoint in package.json scripts, but the actual file lives at `src/frontend/web/src/main.jsx`. The esbuild CLI command in Section 1 uses the full path, but the npm script in M2.5 uses a relative path. Since package.json lives in `src/frontend/web/`, the correct entrypoint is `src/main.jsx` relative to that directory — BUT the `--outdir=dist` is also relative, which is correct. However, the production command in Section 1 uses absolute repo-relative paths (`src/frontend/web/src/main.jsx`, `src/frontend/web/dist`). These two invocation patterns are inconsistent and will cause CI/CD confusion about working directory expectations.
objections.suggested_fix: Standardize: package.json scripts use paths relative to `src/frontend/web/` (where package.json lives). The Section 1 'production command' and 'development command' blocks should explicitly state `cd src/frontend/web && npx esbuild src/main.jsx ...` or simply reference `npm run build` from that directory. Remove the absolute-path versions or label them clearly as 'if running from repo root'.

objections.description: React's useState does NOT trigger re-renders when you mutate a Set in place. The implementation note says `add tab to mountedTabs Set if not present, then toggle visibility` but doesn't specify the immutable update pattern. A naive `mountedTabs.add('search')` won't re-render. This will manifest as: user taps Search tab, nothing mounts, no error thrown. Silent failure.
objections.suggested_fix: Specify the exact state update pattern: `setMountedTabs(prev => prev.has(tabId) ? prev : new Set([...prev, tabId]))`. This creates a new Set reference, triggering the re-render. Alternatively, use an object `{ home: true, search: false, library: false }` which is more idiomatic for React state and avoids the Set serialization footgun entirely.

objections.description: The spec mentions 'visibility-aware 30s poll' for StatusCard and '15s retry interval' for ConnectivityBanner but never defines the visibility-awareness implementation. Is this Page Visibility API? IntersectionObserver? Both? What happens when the tab is backgrounded — does polling stop? What happens on tab-panel hide (display:none + inert) — does a StatusCard on the Home tab keep polling when the user switches to Search? display:none elements are NOT reported as invisible by IntersectionObserver (they're removed from the rendering tree entirely, so IO never fires for them). This needs an explicit implementation contract.
objections.suggested_fix: Define: (1) Use Page Visibility API (`document.visibilitychange`) to pause ALL polling when browser tab is backgrounded. (2) Use a custom `useTabActive(tabId)` hook that reads from Shell's active tab state to pause polling when the React tab panel is hidden. IntersectionObserver is the wrong tool here because display:none bypasses it. (3) On resume: do NOT immediately fire — wait for the next interval tick. This prevents thundering herd on tab-foreground.

objections.description: The spec specifies Inter in 3 weights (regular, medium, semibold) as .woff2 files. Inter Regular is ~96KB, Medium ~97KB, Semibold ~98KB uncompressed (~38-40KB each gzipped). That's ~115KB gzipped for fonts alone. The CSS budget is listed as ~4KB but the font budget is completely absent. Fonts are the single largest asset category and will dominate LCP on 3G. The spec's LCP target of <2.5s on 3G is unachievable with 3 unconstrained font files.
objections.suggested_fix: Subset the fonts. Inter full Latin is ~96KB per weight; subsetting to Basic Latin + Latin-1 Supplement (U+0000-00FF) brings each weight to ~15-18KB (~6-7KB gzipped). Add a font subsetting step to the build: `pyftsubset inter-regular.woff2 --unicodes='U+0000-00FF,U+2010-2027' --flavor=woff2`. Add font budget to the budget table: ≤25KB gzipped total for all 3 weights. If subsetting tooling is out of scope for v1, reduce to 2 weights (regular + semibold — drop medium) and use font-weight numeric values to fake medium via font-synthesis.

objections.location: Section 3 — Bottom sheet, focus trap + portal
objections.description: The spec says bottom sheet uses a portal to document.body, and the focus trap is inside the sheet. But the `inert` management strategy says 'TabBar AND <main> receive inert'. If the portal renders OUTSIDE <main> (which it does — it's on document.body), and you're setting inert on <main>, the portal content is NOT inside an inert subtree, which is correct. But the legacy fallback says 'add aria-hidden to all direct children of <body> except the portal root'. This requires knowing the portal root's DOM identity at the point where you're iterating body children. The spec doesn't define the portal root element's ID or how the inert-management code identifies it.
objections.suggested_fix:
Define: index.html includes `
` as a sibling to `
`. Portal component renders into #portal-root. Inert management iterates `document.body.children`, skips `#portal-root`, sets aria-hidden on everything else. This is deterministic and doesn't require runtime portal detection.

objections.description: The spec says tab switches use `history.replaceState()` to avoid history pollution. But with hash-based routing, the actual navigation is changing `location.hash`. Directly setting `location.hash = '#/search'` triggers a hashchange event AND pushes a history entry. You MUST use `history.replaceState(null, '', '#/search')` explicitly — setting location.hash is NOT equivalent. The spec should call this out explicitly to prevent the most common implementation mistake.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a boxed warning in the navigation.jsx contract: 'NEVER set location.hash directly for tab switches. Always use history.replaceState(state, "", "#" + path). Setting location.hash pushes to history stack, defeating the replace semantics.' Add this as a code comment in the navigate/replace function.

suggestions.description: Add a Step M0: run the existing Alpine/Tailwind index.html and screenshot or list all visible features/interactions. This becomes the regression checklist for the new implementation. Without it, there's no way to verify feature parity after migration. A 10-minute manual inventory prevents 10 hours of 'wait, didn't the old version do X?'

Add eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y to devDependencies. The spec is extremely thorough on accessibility requirements, but none of them are enforced at lint time. jsx-a11y catches missing aria-labels, invalid roles, missing alt text, and non-interactive element event handlers at build time. This is the cheapest accessibility enforcement available.

ErrorBoundary must be a class component (React 18 has no hook equivalent for componentDidCatch / getDerivedStateFromError). The file layout lists it alongside functional components with no callout. Add a comment in the file layout: 'Class component — React requirement for error boundaries. No hooks equivalent exists.' This prevents a future contributor from 'modernizing' it into a broken functional component.

SearchComposer input debounce is specified as 200ms in Section 4's component tree comment, but URL sync debounce is 300ms in Section 3's 'Manual search URL sync' paragraph. These should either be the same value (fetch and URL update happen together) or the spec should explicitly state why they differ (e.g., URL updates are more expensive due to history API calls). I'd recommend 300ms for both — 200ms is too aggressive on slow 3G connections and will fire requests that get immediately aborted.

The spec references a `scripts/check-bundle-size.js` that parses meta.json but never specifies the implementation. It's 15 lines of code — include it in the spec: read meta.json, sum outputs[*].bytes, gzip via zlib.gzipSync, compare to 80KB threshold, exit(1) if over. Without this, someone will write it wrong (e.g., checking uncompressed size, or checking a single file instead of total output).
